trigger: Scott Alexander blog post declaring victory on compositionality bet using Google Imagen
quote: nobody has yet publicly demonstrated a machine that can relate the meanings of sentences to their parts the way a five-year-old child can
claim: No AI system has yet demonstrated human-child-level compositional understanding of language

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post declaring victory on compositionality bet using Google Imagen
quote: the dirty secret in current AI is that for all the bloviating about how current systems have mastered human language, they are still really weak on compositionality, which most linguists (since Frege, over a century ago) would agree is at the very core of how language works
claim: Current AI systems are fundamentally weak at compositionality, which is the core of how language works

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post declaring victory on compositionality bet using Google Imagen
quote: many people have claimed over the years to solve the problem, and none of those systems have proven to be reliable; every proposed solution has been like Clever Hans, working in dim light, leveraging large databases to some degree, but falling apart upon careful inspection
claim: Every claimed solution to compositionality in neural networks has fallen apart upon careful inspection

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post declaring victory on compositionality bet using Google Imagen
quote: If another AI winter does comes, it not be because AI is impossible, but because AI hype exceeds reality
claim: A potential future AI winter would be caused by hype exceeding reality, not by AI being impossible

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post declaring victory on compositionality bet using Google Imagen
quote: Compositionality *is* the wall
claim: Compositionality is the fundamental barrier (wall) for current AI progress

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post declaring victory on compositionality bet using Google Imagen
quote: They probably tried and failed, and don't want to admit it
claim: Google likely tested Imagen on the Winoground compositionality benchmark and failed, and chose not to disclose the results

quote: Can we really say that a system that doesn't understand what wheels are—or what they are for—is a major advance towards artificial intelligence?
claim: Image generation systems like DALL-E and Stable Diffusion do not understand the function of objects they depict, such as what wheels are for

quote: they do well on some inputs (like astronaut rides horse) but more poorly on others (like horse rides astronaut)
claim: Text-to-image systems have inconsistent ability to correlate linguistic input with image output, succeeding on some prompts and failing on others

quote: if all the systems can do is in a hit-or-miss yet spectacular way convert many sentences into text, they may revolutionize the practice of art, but still not really speak to general intelligence, or even represent progress towards general intelligence
claim: Text-to-image AI systems may revolutionize art but do not represent progress toward general intelligence

quote: Nearly everything LeCun said, I had said earlier, some almost word for word—most of it in a 2018 paper called Deep Learning: A Critical Appraisal
claim: LeCun's 2022 claims about AI limitations closely mirror Marcus's 2018 arguments in Deep Learning: A Critical Appraisal

quote: Waiting for cognitive models and reasoning to magically emerge from larger and larger [language] training corpora is like waiting for a miracle
claim: Cognitive models and reasoning will not emerge from simply scaling up language training corpora

quote: A system like GPT-2, for instance, does what it does, for better and for worse, without any explicit (in the sense of directly represented and readily shared) common sense knowledge, without any explicit reasoning, and without any explicit cognitive models of the world it that tries to discuss
claim: Systems like GPT-2 lack explicit common sense knowledge, explicit reasoning, and explicit cognitive models of the world

quote: The most important concern is that robotics is always harder than people think it is
claim: Robotics is consistently harder than people anticipate

quote: techniques that apply to driverless cars (which mostly have to deal with that roads are governed by more or less the same rules wherever you) may have little value in the varied environments of human homes
claim: Self-driving car techniques may not transfer to domestic robotics because homes are far more varied and unpredictable than roads

quote: The real problem w robots has never been demos, anyway (there have been many, for decades); it's making robots in robust in the real world
claim: The core challenge in robotics is robustness in real-world conditions, not staged demonstrations

trigger: Erik Hoel Substack post criticizing AI critics
quote: The problem for the neural network community is not that I have changed my goal posts, it's that my 2012 concerns still stand
claim: The fundamental AI challenges Marcus identified in 2012 (lack of causal reasoning, abstract understanding, logical inference, integration of abstract knowledge) remain unsolved

trigger: Erik Hoel Substack post criticizing AI critics
quote: apparently successful performance on logic by neural networks is often driven by unstable statistical contingency
claim: Neural network performance on logic benchmarks is often driven by statistical artifacts rather than genuine reasoning ability

trigger: Erik Hoel Substack post criticizing AI critics
quote: A growing body of evidence shows that state-of-the-art models learn to exploit spurious statistical patterns in datasets... instead of learning meaning in the flexible and generalizable way that humans do
claim: State-of-the-art AI models exploit spurious statistical patterns rather than learning meaning in a flexible and generalizable way

quote: Eleven years later, comprehension is still lacking and very few if any jobs have actually been replaced by AI
claim: As of 2022, AI has not achieved genuine comprehension and very few jobs have been replaced by AI
target: AI capabilities and labor market impact

quote: Augmentation is way easier, because you don't have to solve the whole job
claim: AI augmentation of human workers is fundamentally easier than full replacement because it does not require solving the entire job

quote: We can't really build humanoid domestic robots without general intelligence, and we don't yet have any serious candidates
claim: Humanoid domestic robots require general intelligence to be safe and useful, and no serious candidate for such intelligence currently exists

quote: the amount of variability explained is not R but rather R squared. So if you have a correlation of R = .5, you actually "explain" (really, just "predict") only 25% of the variance
claim: The Nature neuroscience paper on GPT-2 and brain activity incorrectly reported explained variance, overstating the link between deep learning models and human brain activity

quote: What I didn't see last night was vision
claim: Tesla's Optimus demo lacked both a clear product vision and a strategy for the cognitive AI required for real-world robotics

quote: AI needs genuine, paradigm-shift level innovation. Simply building ever bigger neural networks won't cut it
claim: AI requires paradigm-shift level innovation; simply scaling neural networks will not suffice for AGI

quote: addressing common sense is the single biggest challenge facing robotics; without a solution to it, you cannot have humanoid robots in the home and expect them to be safe
claim: Common sense is the single biggest challenge facing robotics, and without solving it, home humanoid robots cannot be safe

quote: Sooner or later he will recognize that humanoid robots, too, pose a whole set of challenges that lie well beyond our current grasp
claim: Humanoid robotics poses challenges that lie well beyond current AI capabilities

quote: it's kind of scary seeing an LLM confabulate math and science. High school students will love it, and use it to fool and intimidate (some of) their teachers. The rest of us should be terrified
claim: LLMs that confabulate math and science pose a serious risk because students will use them to produce convincing but false academic work

quote: Is this really what AI has come to, automatically mixing reality with bullshit so finely we can no longer recognize the difference?
claim: LLMs like Galactica mix real and fabricated information so seamlessly that it becomes difficult to distinguish truth from falsehood

quote: The problem is about to get much, much worse. Knockoffs of GPT-3 are getting cheaper and cheaper, which means that the cost of generating misinformation is going to zero, and the quantity of misinformation is going to rise—probably exponentially
claim: The declining cost of LLM knockoffs will cause the quantity of machine-generated misinformation to rise exponentially

quote: Large language models are without equal at generating misinformation, but they suck at detecting it
claim: Large language models are effective at generating misinformation but poor at detecting it

quote: Accuracy depends on some reference standard of truth, and they have no way of reasoning about truth. Scaling won't solve this
claim: LLMs have no way of reasoning about truth, and scaling will not solve this fundamental limitation

quote: LLMs are inherently unreliable
claim: Large language models are inherently unreliable

quote: LLMs are unruly beasts; nobody knows how to make them refrain 100% of time from insulting users, giving bad advice, or just plain making stuff up
claim: No one knows how to reliably prevent LLMs from generating insults, bad advice, or fabricated information

quote: LLMs spit our words, not actions (and not API calls either). When an LLM produces a sentence, you can't directly use that sentence to control stuff, unless you build another system to parse the sentences into actions. Nobody knows how to do this reliably, either
claim: LLMs produce text but not actionable commands, and no one knows how to reliably translate LLM output into actions

quote: From the outset Large Language Models like GPT-3 have great at generating surrealist prose, and they can beat a lot of benchmarks, but they are not (and may never be) great tech for reliably inferring user intent from what users say
claim: LLMs may never be reliable technology for inferring user intent

quote: Turning LLMs into a product that controls your home and talks to you in a way that would be reliable enough to use at scale in millions of homes is still a long, long way away
claim: Turning LLMs into reliable home-control products at consumer scale is still a long way from being achieved

quote: Cicero relies quite heavily on hand-crafting, both in the data sets, and in the architecture; in this sense it is in many ways more reminiscent of classical "Good Old Fashioned AI" than deep learning systems
claim: Cicero's success relies heavily on hand-crafted architecture and data, more resembling classical AI than pure deep learning

quote: it's less clear to us how generalizable the particulars of Cicero are
claim: Cicero's specific techniques may have limited generalizability to other tasks beyond Diplomacy

quote: None of this suggests an easy road adapting Cicero to other tasks
claim: Adapting Cicero to tasks beyond Diplomacy would be very difficult

quote: machine learning may ultimately prove to be even more valuable if it is embedded in highly structured systems, with a fair amount of innate, sometimes neurosymbolic machinery
claim: Machine learning will be most valuable when embedded in highly structured systems with innate neurosymbolic components

quote: There is far more innateness here than we have typically seen in recent AI systems
claim: Cicero uses far more innate structure and prior knowledge than typical recent AI systems

quote: There might not be enough data in the world to make scaling maximalism work
claim: There may not be enough data in the world for the scaling maximalism approach to succeed

quote: There might not be enough available compute in the world to make scaling maximalism feasible
claim: There may not be enough available compute in the world to make scaling maximalism feasible

quote: Some important tasks might simply not scale
claim: Some important AI tasks may simply not improve with increased scale

quote: asymptotic 80% performance on even a single important task like this might spell game over for scaling
claim: If scaling plateaus at 80% on even one important task like pragmatic language understanding, the scaling maximalism approach fails

quote: I stand by my prediction that it won't get us to AGI
claim: Scaling maximalism will not get us to AGI

quote: Moore's Law didn't carry us as far and as fast as some people initially hoped, because it is not actually a causal law of the universe. Scaling maximalism is an interesting hypothesis, but I stand by my prediction that it won't get us to AGI
claim: Like Moore's Law, scaling laws in AI are empirical observations, not causal laws, and will not carry AI all the way to AGI

quote: if we want to reach AGI we shouldn't keep putting so many eggs in the scaling-über-alles basket
claim: The AI field is over-investing in scaling approaches at the expense of other approaches needed for AGI

trigger: ChatGPT release and Meta Galactica release
quote: these systems are inherently unreliable, frequently making errors of both reasoning and fact, and prone to hallucination
claim: LLMs like ChatGPT are inherently unreliable, frequently making errors of reasoning and fact, and prone to hallucination

trigger: ChatGPT release and Meta Galactica release
quote: they can easily be automated to generate misinformation at unprecedented scale
claim: LLMs can be automated to generate misinformation at unprecedented scale

trigger: ChatGPT release and Meta Galactica release
quote: they cost almost nothing to operate, and so they are on a path to reducing the cost of generating disinformation to zero
claim: The cost of generating AI-powered disinformation is approaching zero

trigger: ChatGPT release and Meta Galactica release
quote: Nation-states and other bad actors that deliberately produce propaganda, are highly unlikely to voluntarily put down their new arms. Instead, they are likely to use large language models as a new class of automatic weapons, in their war on truth
claim: Nation-states and bad actors will use LLMs as weapons for propaganda and misinformation at unprecedented volume

trigger: ChatGPT release and Meta Galactica release
quote: every social media company and search engine should support and extend StackOverflow's ban; automatically-generated content that is misleading, should not be welcome
claim: Social media companies and search engines should ban misleading AI-generated content

trigger: ChatGPT release and Meta Galactica release
quote: every country is going to need to reconsider its policies on misinformation
claim: Every country needs to reconsider its misinformation policies in light of AI-generated content

trigger: ChatGPT release and Meta Galactica release
quote: provenance is more important now than ever before. User accounts must be more strenuously validated, and new systems like Harvard and Mozilla's human-ID.org that allow for anonymous, bot-resistant authentication need to become mandatory
claim: Bot-resistant authentication systems should become mandatory for online accounts

trigger: ChatGPT release and Meta Galactica release
quote: we are going to need to build a new kind of AI to fight what has been unleashed. Large language models are great at generating misinformation, but poor at fighting it
claim: Countering AI-generated misinformation will require a new kind of AI, because LLMs are good at generating misinformation but poor at detecting it

trigger: ChatGPT release and Meta Galactica release
quote: Large language models lack mechanisms for verifying truth; we need to find new ways to integrate them with the tools of classical AI, such as databases, webs of knowledge, and reasoning
claim: LLMs lack truth verification mechanisms and need to be integrated with classical AI tools like databases, knowledge graphs, and reasoning systems

quote: Not one speaker thought that current AI was anything like the holy grail of artificial general intelligence
claim: A broad cross-section of AI field leaders in 2022 agreed that current AI is not close to artificial general intelligence

quote: ChatGPT, like earlier, related systems is still not reliable, still doesn't understand the physical world, still doesn't understand the psychological world and still hallucinates
claim: ChatGPT is unreliable, does not understand the physical or psychological world, and hallucinates

quote: What good is a search engine that makes stuff up?
claim: LLM-based search engines are fundamentally flawed because they fabricate information

quote: Large language models are not databases. They are glommers-together-of-bits-that don't always belong together
claim: LLMs are text predictors that combine information fragments, not databases, and they frequently combine information that does not belong together

quote: large language models are also wicked hard to update, typically requiring full retraining, sometimes taking weeks or months
claim: LLMs are extremely difficult to update, typically requiring full retraining that takes weeks or months

quote: Driverless cars have taken many years longer than we were originally told; outliers (AKA edge cases) have, at least so far, prevented them from making the transition from demo to widely-available reality. In the quest for a newfangled search I suspect we are once again in for a rough ride
claim: LLM-powered search will face a long and difficult transition from demo to reliable product, similar to driverless cars

quote: It is insidious the way that truth and falsity are so thoroughly and authoritatively mixed together
claim: LLM-powered search engines mix true and false information in an insidiously authoritative way

quote: I personally think the latter, less rosy picture, is closer to the mark
claim: ChatGPT is far from AGI, and the less optimistic assessment of OpenAI's technology is more accurate

quote: large language models themselves are quickly becoming commodities; it's not clear that there is any unique, protectable intellectual property here
claim: Large language models are becoming commodities with no unique protectable intellectual property

quote: If Altman genuinely thinks OpenAI is close to AGI, why is the company preparing to get out and take so much money off the table, when in principle they might be on the verge of owning the world?
claim: OpenAI leadership's financial behavior (selling stakes, ceding profit shares) is inconsistent with a genuine belief that they are close to AGI

quote: Although ChatGPT can write about anything, it is also easily confused
claim: ChatGPT is easily confused despite its broad capabilities

quote: As far as I can tell from rumors, GPT-4 is architecturally essentially the same as GPT-3. If so, we can expect that approach to still be marred by something fundamental: an inability to construct internal models of how the world works
claim: GPT-4 will still lack the ability to construct internal models of how the world works, because it is architecturally similar to GPT-3

quote: GPT-4 will still, like its predecessors, be a bull in a china shop, reckless and hard to control
claim: GPT-4 will be reckless and hard to control, like its predecessors

quote: It will still make a significant number of shake-your-head stupid errors, in ways that are hard to fully predict
claim: GPT-4 will still make a significant number of obviously stupid errors that are hard to predict in advance

quote: Reasoning about physical, psychological and mathematical world will still be unreliable
claim: GPT-4's reasoning about the physical, psychological, and mathematical world will still be unreliable

quote: It will not be trustworthy and complete enough to give reliable medical advice, despite devouring a large fraction of the Internet
claim: GPT-4 will not be trustworthy enough to give reliable medical advice

quote: Fluent hallucinations will still be common, and easily induced, continuing—and in in fact escalating— the risk of large language models being used as a tool for creating plausible-sounding yet false misinformation
claim: GPT-4 will still commonly produce fluent hallucinations, escalating the misinformation risk

quote: Its natural language output still won't be something that one can reliably hook up to downstream programs
claim: GPT-4's output will still not be reliably usable as input for downstream programs or APIs

quote: GPT-4 by itself won't be a general purpose artificial general intelligence capable of taking on arbitrary tasks
claim: GPT-4 will not be a general-purpose AGI capable of taking on arbitrary tasks

quote: "Alignment" between what humans want and what machines do will continue to be a critical, unsolved problem
claim: Alignment between human intent and machine behavior will remain a critical unsolved problem with GPT-4

quote: "Scaling" alone—building bigger and models until they absorb the entire internet — will prove useful, but only to a point
claim: Scaling LLMs will prove useful but insufficient for achieving AGI

quote: Trustworthy, general artificial intelligence, aligned with human values, will come, when it does, from systems that are more structured, with more built-in knowledge, and will incorporate at least some degree of explicit tools for reasoning and planning
claim: Trustworthy AGI will require more structured systems with built-in knowledge and explicit reasoning and planning tools, not just scaled LLMs

quote: Within a decade, maybe much less, the focus of AI will move from a pure focus on scaling large language models to a focus on integrating them with a wide range of other techniques
claim: Within a decade, AI research focus will shift from pure LLM scaling to integrating LLMs with other techniques

quote: In retrospectives written in 2043, intellectual historians will conclude that there was an initial overemphasis on large language models, and a gradual but critical shift of the pendulum back to more structured systems with deeper comprehension
claim: By 2043, historians will conclude there was an overemphasis on LLMs and a shift back to more structured AI systems

trigger: Multiple AI industry embarrassments in January 2023
quote: Real AI wouldn't need sweatshops
claim: Truly intelligent AI would not require massive low-paid human labor for safety filtering

trigger: Multiple AI industry embarrassments in January 2023
quote: Real AI would learn with the efficiency of human children
claim: Truly intelligent AI would learn with the data efficiency of human children rather than requiring massive datasets

trigger: Multiple AI industry embarrassments in January 2023
quote: Real AI would be able to reason from first principles, and generalize far more deeply
claim: Truly intelligent AI would reason from first principles and generalize far more deeply than current systems

trigger: Multiple AI industry embarrassments in January 2023
quote: AI Sweatshops are the consequence of trying to substitute shallow data for deep understanding
claim: The need for low-paid human labor to filter AI outputs is a consequence of substituting shallow data-driven approaches for deep understanding

trigger: Multiple AI industry embarrassments in January 2023
quote: I predicted that 2023 see the first death attributable to large language models, and I stand by that prediction
claim: The first death attributable to large language models will occur in 2023

trigger: Grady Booch tweet that AGI will not happen in your lifetime
quote: I do think we will eventually reach AGI (artificial general intelligence), and quite possibly before the end of this century
claim: AGI will eventually be achieved, quite possibly before the end of this century

trigger: Grady Booch tweet that AGI will not happen in your lifetime
quote: Neither one of us believes for a minute that large language models are remotely close to artificial general intelligence, and what's more, we mostly agree about what's missing: semantics, reasoning, common sense, theory of mind and so on
claim: LLMs are not remotely close to AGI, missing semantics, reasoning, common sense, and theory of mind

trigger: Grady Booch tweet that AGI will not happen in your lifetime
quote: I think we are wasting funding and bright young minds on an approach that probably isn't on the right path
claim: Current AI funding and talent is being wasted on approaches (LLMs) that are probably not on the right path to AGI

trigger: Grady Booch tweet that AGI will not happen in your lifetime
quote: Our data problems are solved, our compute problems are mostly solved; it's now mostly a matter of software, and of rethinking how we build AI
claim: The main remaining barrier to AGI is software architecture and rethinking AI approaches, not data or compute

trigger: Grady Booch tweet that AGI will not happen in your lifetime
quote: most of the money right now is not going to the wrong place: it's mostly going to large language models, and for you, like for me, that just seems like an approximation to intelligence, not the real thing, and as such as frustrating distraction
claim: Most current AI investment goes to LLMs, which are an approximation to intelligence rather than the real thing, and a frustrating distraction from genuine AGI research

trigger: Grady Booch tweet that AGI will not happen in your lifetime
quote: I don't think we will waste more than 5 years before a substantial fraction of AI effort is looking outside the limited streetlight that currently are sucking up so much mental energy
claim: Within 5 years, a substantial fraction of AI research effort will shift away from pure LLM approaches

trigger: Grady Booch tweet that AGI will not happen in your lifetime
quote: in achieving AGI we will have no choice but to find ways of integrating whatever we can get out of deep learning with explicit knowledge and explicit cognitive models
claim: Achieving AGI will require integrating deep learning with explicit knowledge and explicit cognitive models

trigger: Grady Booch tweet that AGI will not happen in your lifetime
quote: we can't say with a straight face that we have achieved the integration we need if a system can't even check its output against Wikipedia or the AP News Wire
claim: Current AI systems cannot even check their output against basic reference sources like Wikipedia, demonstrating a fundamental integration failure

trigger: Grady Booch tweet that AGI will not happen in your lifetime
quote: ChatGPT is (at least in many ways) a far inferior intelligence, that, despite having some undeniable talents, cannot at all be trusted
claim: ChatGPT is a far inferior intelligence that cannot be trusted despite having some genuine talents

trigger: Grady Booch tweet that AGI will not happen in your lifetime
quote: I think that better AI, in which we could directly instill values, might actually make us safer than the world in which we inhabit now
claim: Better AI with directly instilled values could make us safer than the current world

quote: Sentience in large part is about self knowledge, an understanding of the nature of oneself and your relations to others. I'm not sure I've seen any evidence that Galactica has that kind of holistic understanding of what it is
claim: There is no evidence that LLMs like Galactica have self-knowledge or understanding of their own nature, a key component of sentience
